response_text:
# The Thirty-Minute Break

The foreman's name was Dale, and the first thing he told me was to slow down.

I had just stacked forty-two boxes in twelve minutes. I know because I counted, the way you count everything when you are twenty-three and trying to prove something in a new place. The boxes held automotive parts—I never learned exactly which ones—and they weighed roughly thirty pounds each, and I had moved them from a pallet near the loading dock to a staging area along the south wall of the warehouse with what I believed was impressive efficiency. I was sweating through my gray t-shirt. I felt good.

Dale watched me with the patient expression of a man who had seen this particular performance many times. He was maybe fifty-five, with forearms that looked architectural, and he had been with the local for twenty-two years. He pointed at a folding chair near the break table.

"Sit down," he said. "You're on your break."

I told him I had just started. I had been on the floor for less than an hour.

"Doesn't matter," he said. "Break's at ten. It's ten."

---

I had spent the previous four years doing what I'd been doing for most of my working life: non-union jobs that asked everything and promised almost nothing. A restaurant where the owner routinely held our checks until the following week because of what he called "cash flow situations." A moving company that classified us as independent contractors so that a pulled back muscle became entirely our own personal financial problem. A call center where bathroom breaks were timed and managers walked the floor with clipboards, recording how long you'd been away from your headset. In that last place, I once ate a sandwich in four bites while walking back from the men's room because I was afraid of the clipboard.

What those jobs taught me was a specific and very efficient lesson: your time is
